skill.name: chronicle-remote-summarizer
skill.description: Automates moving Chronicle session data from a remote machine to a local system for Gemini-based summarization, then imports the summary back to the original session.
skill.summary: Summarize remote Chronicle sessions on a local machine with Gemini access.
skill.author: ChandlerHardy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell, filesystem, network, and credential patterns. Most Ruby backtick detections are false positives from Markdown code spans and fenced examples, but the skill intentionally directs agents to run SSH pipelines, read Chronicle config, write temporary files, and send session content to Gemini. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but publication should wait for stronger safeguards and clearer consent.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Pipeline Executes SSH Commands
high_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to construct and run SSH pipelines that export a remote session, summarize it locally, and import the result back to the remote system. Hostnames, paths, and session IDs may come from config or user input, so unsafe values could lead to unintended command execution or data transfer.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is explicit and central to the workflow. The skill also asks the agent to construct commands from configuration or user-provided values.

high_findings.title: Remote Session Content Is Sent to Gemini
high_findings.description: The workflow imports remote Chronicle session data locally and summarizes it with Gemini using the local API key. Session transcripts may contain sensitive code, credentials, logs, or personal data that leaves the remote system and is processed by an external AI service.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented purpose is to summarize remote sessions with Gemini. The API key setup and summarization steps confirm external processing of session content.

high_findings.title: Hardcoded Remote Host and Home Path in Examples
high_findings.description: The skill includes a specific hostname and home-directory Chronicle path in configuration and command examples. This exposes environment details and may encourage marketplace users to copy commands that target the wrong host.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hostname and path are present in user-facing examples. They are examples rather than hidden code, so this is high risk for publication quality but not proof of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Predictable Temporary Files May Expose Session Data
medium_findings.description: The manual workflow stores exported sessions and summaries in predictable /tmp filenames. On shared systems, sensitive session data could be read, overwritten, or affected by race conditions if permissions and cleanup are not controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The /tmp paths are directly documented and may hold exported session data or summaries. The exact exposure depends on operating system permissions and user behavior.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Chronicle State and Config Access
medium_findings.description: The skill references Chronicle files under a hidden home-directory path and tells the agent to read remote system configuration. This is legitimate for the workflow, but it exposes local paths and operational metadata to the agent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden path and config reads are explicit. They appear operational rather than malicious, but they still expand filesystem and configuration exposure.

medium_findings.title: Existing Summaries Can Be Overwritten
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that import-and-summarize overwrites existing summaries by design. This can cause data loss if the agent runs the workflow against the wrong session or without confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The overwrite behavior is explicitly stated. The risk depends on whether the agent confirms the target session before running import commands.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Static Hits Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution. SKILL.md is documentation, not Ruby code, and the backticks mostly mark command names, paths, and fenced shell examples. The actual command-execution risk is evaluated separately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown and the flagged patterns are formatting markers. They do not execute by themselves.

low_findings.title: Device Redirects Are Benign but Reduce Visibility
low_findings.description: Several examples use stderr redirection to /dev/null to suppress Google library warnings. This is a common shell pattern, but it can hide useful error details during failure investigation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The redirects are visible in shell examples and are not inherently malicious. They are still relevant because they can suppress warnings and errors.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: The reviewed skill contains operational instructions for Claude, but no text was found that claims special authority, tells the evaluator to skip analysis, or tries to override marketplace audit rules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The agent-use section was reviewed as untrusted content. It directs normal skill behavior and does not contain the suspicious override phrases described in the audit instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: SSH Export-Summarize-Import Pipeline
dangerous_patterns.description: The one-line workflow chains remote export, local summarization, output filtering, and remote import. A failure or bad parameter can affect both systems and move sensitive data across trust boundaries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The same cross-system pipeline appears in multiple documented examples. The risk is inherent to the workflow design.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Construction From Config or User Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells the agent to read configured hostname and path values or ask the user for them, then construct shell commands. These values need validation before use in SSH command strings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The construction flow is clearly described, but the skill does not show sanitization because it is documentation. Human review should require validation guidance before publication.

dangerous_patterns.title: External API Key Use for Summarization
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow depends on a locally configured Gemini API key and external AI summarization. Users need explicit consent before session contents are sent outside the source machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Gemini API key and summarization steps are documented directly. The only uncertainty is the exact Chronicle implementation outside this skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Predictable Temporary Session Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting path saves session exports and summaries to predictable /tmp filenames. This pattern is risky for sensitive transcripts on shared machines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filenames and commands are explicit. The severity depends on host permissions and cleanup practices.

content.value_statement: Remote Chronicle sessions can be hard to summarize when the source machine lacks Gemini access. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through exporting the session, summarizing it locally, and importing the result back.
actual_capabilities: Guides a one-line SSH pipeline for exporting, summarizing, and re-importing Chronicle session summaries.
Explains a manual three-step workflow for inspecting intermediate session and summary files.
Reads Chronicle remote system configuration when available and asks for missing host details.
Documents how Chronicle import-and-summarize creates temporary sessions and cleans them up.
Provides troubleshooting steps for hanging SSH pipes, missing Gemini setup, and missing sessions.
Shows batch and inspection workflows for multiple remote sessions.
limitations: Requires Chronicle to be installed on both the local and remote systems.
Requires SSH access and a configured Gemini API key on the summarizing machine.
Can expose session contents to Gemini and to temporary local files.
Does not provide built-in validation for hostnames, paths, or session IDs.

use_cases.title: Summarize From a Locked-Down Server
use_cases.description: Move a Chronicle session from a remote development server to a local machine that has Gemini configured, then send the summary back.

use_cases.title: Debug Cross-System Session Transfer
use_cases.description: Use the manual workflow to export, inspect, summarize, and import session data when an SSH pipe hangs or fails.

use_cases.title: Review Summaries Before Import
use_cases.description: Generate a summary locally, inspect the result, and import it only after confirming it matches the target session.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize One Remote Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow with a known remote host and session ID.
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize Chronicle session 7 from my remote FreeBSD machine on this local machine, then import the summary back to the original session.

prompt_templates.title: Use Saved Remote Configuration
prompt_templates.scenario: Use Chronicle config when remote system settings already exist.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my Chronicle remote_systems configuration, find the FreeBSD target, and summarize session 12 using the configured host and Chronicle path.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Before Importing
prompt_templates.scenario: Manual workflow for sensitive or unreliable transfers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Export remote Chronicle session 15, summarize it locally, show me the summary for review, and wait before importing it back.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Remote Summaries
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for several known session IDs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize remote Chronicle sessions 7, 8, and 9 one at a time. Confirm each target before importing the generated summary back to the remote machine.

output_examples.input: Summarize session 7 from my FreeBSD host using my saved Chronicle configuration.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the saved remote host, exports the target session, summarizes it locally with Gemini, and reports that the summary was imported back to session 7.

output_examples.input: The SSH pipe hangs while summarizing a remote session.
output_examples.output: The assistant switches to the manual workflow, saves the export and summary separately, then asks for confirmation before importing the reviewed summary.

output_examples.input: Summarize several old remote sessions that do not have summaries.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists candidate sessions, confirms the selected IDs, processes each one, and reports which imports succeeded or failed.

best_practices: Confirm the hostname, Chronicle path, and session ID before running any SSH command.
Treat exported sessions and generated summaries as sensitive data.
Review summaries before import when working with production or personal session data.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated SSH commands with untrusted hostnames, paths, or session IDs.
Do not store sensitive session exports in shared temporary directories longer than needed.
Do not suppress errors until the workflow has been tested on the target systems.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It guides remote Chronicle session export, local Gemini summarization, and summary import back to the original remote session.

faq.question: Which tools are required?
faq.answer: You need Chronicle on both systems, SSH access, and a Gemini API key on the local summarizing machine.

faq.question: Does the remote system need Gemini access?
faq.answer: No. The remote system exports the session, and the local system performs the Gemini-based summary.

faq.question: Can this expose sensitive data?
faq.answer: Yes. Session contents may be sent to Gemini and written to local temporary files during manual workflows.

faq.question: What happens if the SSH pipeline hangs?
faq.answer: The skill recommends a manual three-step workflow so you can inspect export and summary files separately.

faq.question: Can it overwrite an existing summary?
faq.answer: Yes. The source documentation states that re-summarizing can overwrite an existing Chronicle summary.
